headline: Meta (META) Picks CRED Founder Kunal Shah To Run WhatsApp Globally
summary: Meta Platforms (NasdaqGS:META) has appointed CRED founder Kunal Shah as global head of WhatsApp. The move follows Meta's US$900 million investment in Indian fintech firm CRED. The appointment signals a push to expand WhatsApp beyond messaging into digital payments and commerce, with a focus on emerging markets such as India. headline: AbbVie to Host Second-Quarter 2026 Earnings Conference Call

AbbVie will host a live webcast of the earnings conference call at 8 a.m. Central time. It will be accessible through AbbVie's Investor Relations website at investors.abbvie.com. An archived edition of the session will be available later that day.

headline: The Week in Numbers: Micron climbs high, Apple raises prices
summary:
STORY: From Micron's mega market cap to Apple's price hikes...

...this is the Week in Numbers.

:: The Week in Numbers

:: $1.4 trillion.

About $1.4 trillion was Micron's market cap after it briefly blew past the value of Meta and Tesla for the first time.

It came after the firm forecast quarterly profit and revenue well above expectations.

Micron is a major memory-chip maker and a key supplier to AI titan Nvidia.

The company's shares have more than tripled in value so far this year, and soared close to a fifth following its results.

:: 5%

Almost 5% is how far Apple shares fell after it announced price hikes.

The tech giant said its cheapest laptop will jump from $599 to $699, with iPad prices also rising - although the iPhone won't jump for now.

Apple says it can no longer avoid passing on rising costs to consumers.

The price of memory chips jumped by around 98% in the first quarter, and has kept soaring due to the AI boom.

:: 42% 

42% was how much shares of Wendy's soared as retail traders bought up the beaten-down stock in the latest meme-like rally.

Trading on the heavily shorted stock was paused multiple times due to volatility..

Shares briefly hit a more than seven-month high.

The fast food chain is battling weak sales and pressure from an activist investor, with its stock down more than 78% from record highs five years ago.

:: $648 billion

Almost $648 billion is how much Samsung plans to invest in South Korea over 10 years.

That's according to the Maeil Business Newspaper, which said the firm will announce its intentions on Monday.

The investment includes a potential $194 billion to build chip factories in the country's southwest, as well as plans for AI data centers, batteries and displays.

There was no immediate response to a request for comment from Samsung.

:: $606.5 million

And $606.5 million was H&M's operating profit during its fiscal second quarter.

That was smaller than expected for the Swedish fashion retailer.

H&M blamed weaker consumer demand and inflation made worse by the Iran war.

CEO Daniel Erver said Europe, particularly the western part of it, had gone through a difficult quarter.

content.title: The Week in Numbers: Micron climbs high, Apple raises prices

The fast food chain is battling weak sales and pressure from an activist investor, with its stock down more than 78% from record highs five years ago.

The investment includes a potential $194 billion to build chip factories in the country's southwest, as well as plans for AI data centers, batteries and displays.

There was no immediate response to a request for comment from Samsung.

:: $606.5 million

That was smaller than expected for the Swedish fashion retailer.

H&M blamed weaker consumer demand and inflation made worse by the Iran war.

CEO Daniel Erver said Europe, particularly the western part of it, had gone through a difficult quarter.

headline: Does the Clarity Act have a human trafficking blind spot?
summary: CoinDesk's The Policy Protocol hosts Rebecca Rettig and Renato Mariotti open with two hot topics: Coinbase's SEC-registered AI agentic trading launch and tribal gaming operators' letter to Senators Thune and Schumer demanding that the CLARITY Act include a prohibition on prediction markets. Then Katie Boller Gosewisch, Executive Director of the Alliance to End Human Trafficking, joins to challenge CLARITY's Section 604, arguing that shielding DeFi developers from money-transmitter liability creates a "duty of care" gap that traffickers can exploit. Plus, Rebecca and Renato close by naming Meta as their Person of the Week for its reported move into points-based prediction markets.

content.title: Does the Clarity Act have a human trafficking blind spot?
content.description: CoinDesk's The Policy Protocol hosts Rebecca Rettig and Renato Mariotti open with two hot topics: Coinbase's SEC-registered AI agentic trading launch and tribal gaming operators' letter to Senators Thune and Schumer demanding that the CLARITY Act include a prohibition on prediction markets. Then Katie Boller Gosewisch, Executive Director of the Alliance to End Human Trafficking, joins to challenge CLARITY's Section 604, arguing that shielding DeFi developers from money-transmitter liability creates a "duty of care" gap that traffickers can exploit. Plus, Rebecca and Renato close by naming Meta as their Person of the Week for its reported move into points-based prediction markets.
content.summary: CoinDesk's The Policy Protocol hosts Rebecca Rettig and Renato Mariotti open with two hot topics: Coinbase's SEC-registered AI agentic trading launch and tribal gaming operators' letter to Senators Thune and Schumer demanding that the CLARITY Act include a prohibition on prediction markets. Then Katie Boller Gosewisch, Executive Director of the Alliance to End Human Trafficking, joins to challenge CLARITY's Section 604, arguing that shielding DeFi developers from money-transmitter liability creates a "duty of care" gap that traffickers can exploit. Plus, Rebecca and Renato close by naming Meta as their Person of the Week for its reported move into points-based prediction markets.

headline: Zuckerberg asks Meta to explore working with Polymarket and Kalshi, NYT reports
summary: Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg has urged his lieutenants to explore partnerships with the popular prediction markets ‌Polymarket and Kalshi as his company builds a similar ‌app, the New York Times said on Friday, citing three employees with knowledge ​of the matter.  Meta and Kalshi did not immediately respond to requests for comment, while Polymarket declined to comment when contacted by Reuters.  The social media company's executives ‌have said Arena, Meta's ⁠new prediction market app under development, will differ from Polymarket and Kalshi, which accept real-money wagers, because ⁠it will instead rely on video-game-like "points", the report said.
